methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Chips & Semiconductors (found 0 same-stage, 2 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 14 same-sector peers exist in Chips & Semiconductors, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Semiconductors P/S median is 20.3x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

data.narrative:
OBSIDIA Semiconductor is a seed-stage chip design company founded by Erik Hosler and Adam Wilson, competing within a frontier-tech semiconductors sector that encompasses 3 public comparables (ALAB, NVDA, AMD) and 8 private peers including Cerebras, PsiQuantum, Etched, SambaNova Systems, and Credo Semiconductor.

Two standard valuation triangulation methods are not applicable to this engagement. Peer-median benchmarking cannot be used because the narrow peer set—filtered by stage, adjacency, and valuation tier—yields fewer than three datapoints necessary for statistical reliability; while 14 same-sector peers exist in Chips & Semiconductors, none satisfy the filtering criteria. Public-comp anchoring via price-to-sales multiples is similarly inapplicable; the median public semiconductor P/S stands at 20.3x across the three public tickers, but OBSIDIA's revenue is not on file, which is typical for pre-revenue or undisclosed private companies.

Without actionable quantitative inputs from either method, a triangulated valuation range cannot be derived from the data provided. Proceeding would require either disclosure of OBSIDIA's revenue or ARR, a expanded and more granular peer set meeting stage and tier filters, or alternative methodologies such as venture capital method or discounted cash flow—none of which are supported by the current data. The primary valuation uncertainty is the absence of financial disclosure; secondary uncertainty stems from the nascent and heterogeneous nature of frontier-chip ventures, which limits comparable company reliability.
